subject: english
learning_objectives: Define phrasal verbs and explain how meaning is determined
Identify two-word and three-word phrasal verbs
Use common phrasal verbs correctly in sentences

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content: A phrasal verb is a verb combined with one or more adverbial particles to form a multi-word verb. Its meaning cannot be determined from the individual words alone; the whole phrase must be understood as a unit.

sections.heading: Two-Word Phrasal Verbs
sections.content:
turn down – refuse: I turned down the offer.
give in – surrender: Our team refused to give in to their opponents.
run across – meet by chance: We ran across an old friend at Aba.
call off – cancel: The workers called off their strike.
turn up – appear: He turned up as soon as we arrived.
put off – postpone: The election was put off till the next meeting.
come across – meet by chance: We came across Ledogo in the street.
come through – experience: He has come through a lot of hardship.

sections.heading: Three-Word Phrasal Verbs (Double Particles)
sections.content:
These phrasal verbs have two particles; the whole combination has a single meaning.

put up with – tolerate: I cannot put up with his insulting behaviour.
cut down on – reduce: We have been advised to cut down on our expenses.
get away with – escape punishment for: He cannot get away with the crime.
look forward to – anticipate: We look forward to your next visit.
stay away from – avoid: You have been warned to stay away from the building.
